title: Nordic walking

infobox.value: is a [Finnish](/wiki/Finland "Finland")-origin total-body version of [walking](/wiki/Walking "Walking") that can be done both by non-athletes as a health-promoting [physical activity](/wiki/Physical_activity "Physical activity") and by athletes as a sport. The activity is performed with specially designed walking poles similar to [ski poles](/wiki/Ski_poles "Ski poles").

["KOULUTUSAINEISTOA MAURI REPO: SEURAVALMENTAJAN PERUSTIEDOT HIIHDON LAJIOSA"](https://web.archive.org/web/20140101084718/http://www.tul.fi/Portals/0/DOKUMENTIT/Koulutus/Vanhat%20materiaalit/Hiihdon%20lajiosa%201979.pdf) (PDF). *Tul.fi*. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Cross-country walking with poles

[![Nordic walking group](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/6f/Nordic-Walking-naturale.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nordic-Walking-naturale.jpg)

Nordic walkers in a park

**Nordic walking** is a [Finnish](/wiki/Finland "Finland")-origin total-body version of [walking](/wiki/Walking "Walking") that can be done both by non-athletes as a health-promoting [physical activity](/wiki/Physical_activity "Physical activity") and by athletes as a sport. The activity is performed with specially designed walking poles similar to [ski poles](/wiki/Ski_poles "Ski poles").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nordic_walking&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Nordic walking (originally Finnish *sauvakävely*) is fitness walking with specially designed poles. While trekkers, backpackers, and skiers had been using the basic concept for decades, Nordic walking was first formally defined with the publication of "*Hiihdon lajiosa*" (translation: "A part of cross-country skiing training methodic") by [Mauri Repo](/wiki/Mauri_Repo "Mauri Repo") in 1979.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Nordic walking's concept was developed on the basis of off-season ski-training activity done using one-piece ski poles.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

[![A Nordic walking group](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Nordic_Walking_is_enjoyed_by_all.jpg/250px-Nordic_Walking_is_enjoyed_by_all.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nordic_Walking_is_enjoyed_by_all.jpg)

A Nordic walking group

For decades hikers and backpackers used their one-piece ski poles long before trekking and Nordic walking poles came onto the scene. Ski racers deprived of snow have always used and still do use their one-piece ski poles for ski walking and hill bounding. The first poles specially designed and marketed to fitness walkers were produced by Exerstrider of the US in 1988. *Nordic Walker* poles were produced and marketed by [Exel](/wiki/Exel_Composites "Exel Composites") in 1997. Exel coined and popularized the term 'Nordic Walking' in 1999.

## Benefits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nordic_walking&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Benefits")]

Compared to regular walking, Nordic walking (also called **pole walking**) involves applying force to the poles with each stride. Nordic walkers use more of their entire body (with greater intensity) and receive fitness building stimulation not present in normal walking for the chest, [latissimus dorsi muscle](/wiki/Latissimus_dorsi_muscle "Latissimus dorsi muscle"), triceps, biceps, shoulder, abdominals, spinal and other core muscles that may result in significant increases in heart rate at a given pace.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Nordic walking has been estimated as producing up to a 46% increase in energy consumption, compared to walking without poles.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)
Several research studies have been conducted on the effects of Nordic walking. One study in particular compared regular [walking](/wiki/Walking "Walking") to Nordic walking.[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6) This study showed that Nordic walking led to more significant decreases in [BMI](/wiki/Body_mass_index "Body mass index") and [waist circumference](/wiki/Waist_circumference "Waist circumference") compared to regular walking.[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6) The Nordic walking group was the only group that experienced reductions in total body fat and increases in [aerobic capacity](/wiki/VO2_max "VO2 max").[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6) Another research study that was done compared the effects of Nordic walking, conventional walking, and [resistance-band](/wiki/Resistance_band "Resistance band") training on overall [fitness](/wiki/Physical_fitness "Physical fitness") in older adults.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7) The study concluded that while all three methods are useful for improving overall fitness, Nordic walking is the most effective.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7) Research[[8]](#cite_note-8) also shows that the better your technique, the greater the benefits with the [INWA](https://www.inwa-nordicwalking.com/) technique being most effective. [Harvard Medical School](/wiki/Harvard_Medical_School "Harvard Medical School") has evidence that shows Nordic walking has more [calorie](/wiki/Calorie "Calorie") burning characteristics than regular walking.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Equipment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nordic_walking&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Equipment")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/%D0%A0%D1%83%D0%BA%D0%BE%D1%8F%D1%82%D1%8C_%D0%B8_%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BC%D0%BB%D1%8F%D0%BA_%D0%BF%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%BE%D0%BA_%D0%B4%D0%BB%D1%8F_%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B0%D0%B2%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B9_%D1%85%D0%BE%D0%B4%D1%8C%D0%B1%D1%8B.jpg/250px-%D0%A0%D1%83%D0%BA%D0%BE%D1%8F%D1%82%D1%8C_%D0%B8_%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BC%D0%BB%D1%8F%D0%BA_%D0%BF%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%BE%D0%BA_%D0%B4%D0%BB%D1%8F_%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B0%D0%B2%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B9_%D1%85%D0%BE%D0%B4%D1%8C%D0%B1%D1%8B.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A0%D1%83%D0%BA%D0%BE%D1%8F%D1%82%D1%8C_%D0%B8_%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BC%D0%BB%D1%8F%D0%BA_%D0%BF%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%BE%D0%BA_%D0%B4%D0%BB%D1%8F_%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B0%D0%B2%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B9_%D1%85%D0%BE%D0%B4%D1%8C%D0%B1%D1%8B.jpg)

The handle of a walking pole

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Push_and_Pull.jpg/250px-Push_and_Pull.jpg)](/wiki/File:Push_and_Pull.jpg)

Spike for offroad and rubber for asphalt

Nordic walking poles are significantly shorter than those recommended for cross-country skiing. Nordic walking poles come in one-piece, non-adjustable shaft versions, available in varying lengths, and telescoping two or three piece twist-locking versions of adjustable length. One piece poles are generally stronger and lighter, but must be matched to the user. Telescoping poles are 'one-size fits all', and are more transportable.

Nordic walking poles feature a range of grips and wrist-straps, or rarely, no wrist-strap at all. The straps eliminate the need to tightly grasp the grips.
As with many trekking poles, Nordic walking poles come with removable [rubber](/wiki/Natural_rubber "Natural rubber") tips for use on hard surfaces and hardened metal tips for trails, the beach, snow and ice. Most poles are made from lightweight [aluminium](/wiki/Aluminium "Aluminium"), [carbon fiber](/wiki/Carbon-fiber-reinforced_polymer "Carbon-fiber-reinforced polymer"), or [composite materials](/wiki/Composite_material "Composite material"). Special walking shoes are not required, although there are shoes being marketed as specifically designed for the sport.

## Technique

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nordic_walking&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Technique")]

The cadences of the arms, legs and body are, rhythmically speaking, similar to those used in normal, vigorous, walking. The range of arm movement regulates the length of the stride. Restricted arm movements will mean a natural restricted pelvic motion and stride length. The longer the pole thrust, the longer the stride and more powerful the swing of the pelvis and upper torso.

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nordic_walking&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Trekking pole](/wiki/Trekking_pole "Trekking pole")
* [Walking stick](/wiki/Walking_stick "Walking stick")
* [Cross country running](/wiki/Cross_country_running "Cross country running")
